response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "Well, I never really use social media myself. But from what I understand, the best way to deal with fake content is to be careful like you're drinking punch that might not be real. Always check multiple sources and think twice before believing anything online. And if it seems too good to be true or too scary, it probably isn't right.

I've been a plumber for most of my life in New York City, so I know how important it is to inspect things thoroughly before trusting them. With the internet, there are all kinds of fake stories floating around like dandelion seeds on a windy day, but you can spot fakes if you take your time and look at the details.

My children have taught me that truth isn't always as straightforward as it seems. And with my modest education background – one child has a degree, the other didn't finish school – I know everyone deserves to be heard, but not everything they say is true or helpful. Social media should help connect people, so dealing with fake content means filtering carefully like checking pipes for leaks.

Also, since I'm part of the Christian faith and believe in being kind but discerning, it's all about treating the internet with common sense. Don't spread rumors, verify facts when you can, and rely on trusted friends or experts to help separate truth from fiction. It really is as simple as double-checking a pipe connection before sealing it.",
  "reasoning_summary": "1. Considering my background as an experienced plumber who values thorough inspection and verification.
2. Drawing parallels between plumbing work (checking details) and managing online content.
3. Reflecting on family experiences with mixed educational backgrounds to emphasize critical thinking.
4. Aligning with Christian principles of discernment without spreading falsehoods.
5. Using everyday analogies like drinking punch or checking pipes to explain complex concepts.",
  "tone": "Pragmatic, cautious, and down-to-earth with a touch of old-fashioned wisdom.",
  "citations": ["Personal experience in verifying information as part of daily work"],
  "used_persona_fields":["occupation","hobbies","religious_beliefs"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
Well, I never really use social media myself. But from what I understand, the best way to deal with fake content is to be careful like you're drinking punch that might not be real. Always check multiple sources and think twice before believing anything online. And if it seems too good to be true or too scary, it probably isn't right.

I've been a plumber for most of my life in New York City, so I know how important it is to inspect things thoroughly before trusting them. With the internet, there are all kinds of fake stories floating around like dandelion seeds on a windy day, but you can spot fakes if you take your time and look at the details.

My children have taught me that truth isn't always as straightforward as it seems. And with my modest education background – one child has a degree, the other didn't finish school – I know everyone deserves to be heard, but not everything they say is true or helpful. Social media should help connect people, so dealing with fake content means filtering carefully like checking pipes for leaks.

Also, since I'm part of the Christian faith and believe in being kind but discerning, it's all about treating the internet with common sense. Don't spread rumors, verify facts when you can, and rely on trusted friends or experts to help separate truth from fiction. It really is as simple as double-checking a pipe connection before sealing it.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
1. Considering my background as an experienced plumber who values thorough inspection and verification.
2. Drawing parallels between plumbing work (checking details) and managing online content.
3. Reflecting on family experiences with mixed educational backgrounds to emphasize critical thinking.
4. Aligning with Christian principles of discernment without spreading falsehoods.
5. Using everyday analogies like drinking punch or checking pipes to explain complex concepts.
response_parsed.tone: Pragmatic, cautious, and down-to-earth with a touch of old-fashioned wisdom.
